coredata.dc:title: Femtosecond pulse laser fabrication of zinc oxide quantum clusters/nanoparticles and their electrical, optical, and chemical characteristics
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Feyissa Gemeda, Getachew
Hwang, Woei-Jyh
Imae, Toyoko
Yen, Yee-Wen
coredata.dc:description: Despite various studies on the preparation of different types and sizes of ZnO, the synthesis of quantum clusters of bare metal oxide has rarely been reported. The research goals of this study were to create clusters/nanoparticles using femtosecond laser irradiation to increase the electrical, optical, and chemical functionalities of ZnO. Femtosecond pulse laser irradiation deposition technology was used here to produce ZnO from a precursor in water (pH = 5.5) and aqueous alkaline solution (pH = 10.2). These products were named ZnO(F5.5) and ZnO(F10.2), respectively. In this procedure, Zn ions react with hydroxyl radicals (OH*) produced by the decomposition of water molecules, and Zn(OH*)2 is dehydrated by femtosecond laser energy to create ZnO. The spherical particle size of ZnO(F5.5) after 1–30 min irradiation was found to be small (1–7 nm) compared to that of ZnO(F10.2) spheres (10–13 nm). Furthermore, ZnO(F5.5) shows a larger band gap (5.3–5.6 eV), a longer electron life time (40.4 ms), and a higher emission intensity (483 a.u.) compared to ZnO(F10.2). For the photodegradation of harmful pollutants, ZnO(F5.5) prepared at 1 min of laser irradiation reduces formaldehyde by 98.5% under UV light irradiation for 15 min. However, ZnO(F10.2) and other larger ZnO particles with various shapes require a longer time for formaldehyde conversion. These results confirm that an ultrasmall ZnO nanoparticle (1 nm in size) can be called a quantum cluster and has better electrical, optical, and photocatalyst characteristics. In particular, efficient photocatalytic reactions may be used to study the ecological and environmental impacts of ZnO quantum cluster.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Despite various studies on the preparation of different types and sizes of ZnO, the synthesis of quantum clusters of bare metal oxide has rarely been reported. The research goals of this study were to create clusters/nanoparticles using femtosecond laser irradiation to increase the electrical, optical, and chemical functionalities of ZnO. Femtosecond pulse laser irradiation deposition technology was used here to produce ZnO from a precursor in water (pH = 5.5) and aqueous alkaline solution (pH = 10.2). These products were named ZnO(F5.5) and ZnO(F10.2), respectively. In this procedure, Zn ions react with hydroxyl radicals (OH*) produced by the decomposition of water molecules, and Zn(OH*)2 is dehydrated by femtosecond laser energy to create ZnO. The spherical particle size of ZnO(F5.5) after 1–30 min irradiation was found to be small (1–7 nm) compared to that of ZnO(F10.2) spheres (10–13 nm). Furthermore, ZnO(F5.5) shows a larger band gap (5.3–5.6 eV), a longer electron life time (40.4 ms), and a higher emission intensity (483 a.u.) compared to ZnO(F10.2). For the photodegradation of harmful pollutants, ZnO(F5.5) prepared at 1 min of laser irradiation reduces formaldehyde by 98.5% under UV light irradiation for 15 min. However, ZnO(F10.2) and other larger ZnO particles with various shapes require a longer time for formaldehyde conversion. These results confirm that an ultrasmall ZnO nanoparticle (1 nm in size) can be called a quantum cluster and has better electrical, optical, and photocatalyst characteristics. In particular, efficient photocatalytic reactions may be used to study the ecological and environmental impacts of ZnO quantum cluster.

ce:section.ce:para: The size and shape of nanoscale materials allow for excellent control of numerous physical and chemical properties, including electrical and thermal conductivities, magnetic properties, luminescence, and catalytic activity [1]. Chemists and physicists are particularly curious about the synthesis and morphological control of nanosized particles, which exhibit amazing and novel phenomena due to the quantum size effect. Metal oxides can be synthesized using a variety of techniques such as precipitation [2], hydrothermal procedures [3], chemical vapor deposition [4], and mechanical milling [5]. Femtosecond laser processing was first developed in 1987 [6], and it has become an attractive academic research topic and a reliable tool for manufacturing [7,8]. The duration of femtosecond laser pulses ranges from one to many hundreds of femtoseconds. In addition, due to the unique properties based on nonlinear optical effects derived from an ultrashort pulse width and high peak power density, the femtosecond laser field has gained increasing attention in recent decades [9,10]. Particularly, by connecting femtosecond laser technology with nanotechnology, an effective method for producing and modifying nanostructures in microelectronics has been developed [11,12]. When the laser pulse length is smaller than the electron–phonon coupling period, the large amount of energy generated by the pulse can be stored by electrons. A femtosecond laser beam can be utilized to decrease heat impact during material processing. However, a systematic comparison of the synthesis procedure and characteristics of the products has never been previously performed.

ZnO, as an n-type semiconductor metal oxide, is a highly desirable material among useful photocatalysts due to its low cost, availability, and lack of toxicity [13]. Several methods have been used to create ZnO nanoparticles, but most of them have numerous drawbacks, including high temperatures and organic patterns and require a complicated procedure and sophisticated control. Therefore, a simple method to produce and control the structure and size of the products is required. Variables such as particle size, shape, and composition have a great influence on the photocatalytic and photovoltaic properties of ZnO nanoparticles [14-18]. Thus, ZnO is a widely used semiconductor photocatalyst due to its high photosensitivity, wide band gap, strong oxidizing power, nontoxicity, and high photodegradation activity [19]. A reproducible physical approach for the femtosecond pulse laser deposition technique fulfills the present purpose of preparing ZnO quantum clusters or nanoparticles.

In this work, the characteristics of ZnO nanoparticles with different particle sizes and shapes synthesized by different methods were compared. The main focus is the fabrication of cluster-size ZnO nanoparticles using a femtosecond pulse laser irradiation technique for precursor metal ions in water. ZnO nanoparticles were synthesized at different irradiation times and pH values (with and the addition of alkali), and their characteristics were compared with those synthesized using different (conventional) methods, which have different particle sizes and shapes. ZnO clusters/nanoparticles prepared using this physical bottom-up method will minimize the particle size and can exhibit unique physical, chemical, electrical, and optical properties more than ZnO nanoparticles prepared by conventional chemical or mechanical procedures. In particular, a size-dependent enhancement of photocatalytic activity is highly expected. The mechanism of ZnO synthesis by femtosecond laser irradiation reaction is also elucidated.

Poly(amido amine) (PAMAM) dendrimer (ethylenediamine core, generation 4.0 solution, 10 wt% in methanol) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, 98%), formaldehyde (HCHO, 37 wt% in water, with 10–15% methanol as a stabilizer), and 2,4-pentane dione (C5H8O2, acetylacetone) were purchased from Acros Organics (USA). Ammonium acetate (CH3COONH4) was purchased from Panreac, (Spain). Acetic acid (CH3COOH, 99.7%) was purchased from Fisher Scientific (U.K). Sodium hydroxide (95%) was purchased from MACRON fine chemicals (Sweden). Tert-butyl alcohol (99.5%) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-1-piperdinyloxy free radical (TEMPO) (98+%) were purchased from Across Organics (USA). 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO, 98%) was purchased from Columbia (USA). Ultrapure water (resistivity of 18.2 ΜΩcm, Yamato Millipore WT100) was used for the experiments.

Characterization was performed using an atomic force microscope (AFM, Veeco Instruments Inc., USA), a high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F30, Philips, 300 kV), a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, ISM-200FX II, Japan, 120 kV), an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, D2 Phaser, USA), an X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, VG scientific, Model ESCALAB 250, England), an ultraviolet (UV)-visible absorption spectrophotometer (JASCO, V-670, Japan), and a photoluminescence (PL) spectrometer (F-7000, Hitachi High-Technologies Co., Japan). An electrochemical workstation (ZAHNER, model XPot, Germany) was used for electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements and obtaining Mott-Schottky plots in the frequency range from100 mHz to 100 kHz by using a catalyst deposited on an ITO substrate, Ag/AgCl, and platinum wire as working, reference, and counter electrodes, respectively, and an aqueous 1 M NaCl electrolyte solution. A femtosecond laser (Kokyo Inc., Japan) generated by a pulsed laser amplifier with a wavelength of 1550 nm was used with a constant power output of 47 mW.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ZnO by mechanical and hydrothermal methods
ce:section.ce:para:
The synthesis of ZnO by a mechanical method followed a previously described procedure [14]. The product was designated as ZnO(1). The synthesis of ZnO(2)-ZnO(5) by a chemical method in water was carried out by the hydrothermal method under alkaline conditions. ZnO synthesized in water containing a PAMAM dendrimer was named ZnO(6) and ZnO(7), depending on the dendrimer concentration. For the synthesis of ZnO (ZnO(8) and ZnO(9) or ZnO(P10.2)) by the polyol method [15], ethylene glycol was used instead of water. Separately, ZnO was prepared in ethylene glycol without NaOH and called ZnO(P5.5). The details and conditions of synthesis for each ZnO sample are listed in Supporting Information
Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ZnO by a femtosecond pulse laser irradiation method
ce:section.ce:para: Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (1 mM) was dissolved in water (100 ml) by sonication for 30 min. The solution (2 ml) was placed into a 10 mm quartz cell cuvette wherein a freshly cleaved mica substrate was immersed followed by irradiation with a femtosecond laser at different times. The products formed on the mica substrate were dried overnight at room temperature. The product was designated as ZnO(F5.5). An equimolar ratio of zinc nitrate hexahydrate and sodium hydroxide was dissolved separately in 50 ml of water and sonicated for 20 min. Then, the NaOH solution was added dropwise to the zinc nitrate solution until the pH reached a value of 10.2, and the mixture was sonicated for 30 min. The transparent solution (2 ml) was placed into a 10 mm quartz cell cuvette and irradiated with a femtosecond laser at different times. The product was named ZnO(F10.2). The synthesis conditions for ZnO(F5.5) and ZnO(F10.2) are also listed in Table S1. The temperature of the solution (2 ml) in the 10 mm quartz cell cuvette was also measured, and the temperature increase is plotted in Figure S1 . The temperature is increased by 5 °C after 1 min irradiation and 23 °C after 30 min irradiation. However, these temperatures are not high enough for the dehydration of Zn(OH)2 because hydrothermal ZnO preparation requires heating to at least 70–80 °C.

For the active scavenger assays, scavenger reagents (tert-butyl, TEMPO, and DMPO (1 mM, corresponding to 0.961 ml, 1.56 mg, and 1.13 mg, respectively) were separately dissolved in water (10 ml). Then, the scavenger solution (0.1 ml) was added to the Zn ion precursor in water or in aqueous NaOH solution (1.95 ml), and synthesis with femtosecond laser irradiation was performed for 1 min. The analysis of production of ZnO(F5.5) in the reactor including the scavenger was analyzed by AFM observation of the particles deposited on the mica substrate. On the other hand, ZnO(F10.2) was quantified by gravimetric analysis of the particles deposited on the mica.

Quantification of formaldehyde was carried out by colorimetric analysis following a previously reported procedure [20]. The colorimetric medium was prepared by adding acetic acid (0.3 ml) and acetylacetone (0.2 ml) to an aqueous solution of ammonium acetate (30 g) and adjusting the total volume to 200 ml. A formaldehyde (37% in water, 1.5 ml) solution was diluted with water, and a final stock solution of 300 ppm (10-2 M) was prepared. Formaldehyde solutions at five different concentrations were mixed with a colorimetric agent and kept at 42 °C for 30 min. Then, the absorbance at a wavelength of 412 nm for 3,5-diacetyl-1,4-dihydrolutidine (DDL) formed from 2,4-pentanedione, formaldehyde, and ammonia, was measured with a UV–visible absorption spectrometer at a scan rate of 400 nm/min using a 1 cm quartz cell. An aqueous dispersion (1.95 ml) of ZnO (1.63 mg) was mixed with formaldehyde (300 ppm, 0.05 ml). After the mixture (50 ml) was stirred in the dark for 10 min, it was irradiated under stirring with UV light sourced from a 250 W Hg lamp (HOYA-SCHOT EX250 UV light source, JAPAN SERVO Co., Ltd, Japan). The dispersion (2 ml) was recorded at an interval of 2.5 min, mixed with a 1 ml colorimetric agent, and the absorbance at 412 nm in a UV–visible absorption spectrum was measured to determine the remaining formaldehyde content.

ZnO was synthesized using mechanical and chemical methods under various conditions as listed in Table S1. Their morphologies were investigated with TEM and SEM as shown in Fig. 1 and Figure S2. ZnO(1) shows a flower-like structure consisting of nanorods. ZnO(2) shows a plate-like structure, but ZnO(3), which was synthesized at lower precursor concentrations, shows a biconical structure. ZnO(4) was synthesized at the same concentration of zinc nitrate as ZnO(3) but with a different Zn2+:NaOH value, and its structure is a nanosphere. ZnO(5) synthesized at a further diluted precursor concentration in water also shows a biconical shape like ZnO(3) with the same Zn2+:NaOH mole ratio. ZnO(6) and (7) were synthesized under the same conditions (precursor concentration and a mole ratio of Zn+2 and NaOH) as ZnO(5) but with adding protector (dendrimer), but they also show a bicone-based structure, but the average size of the long axis is reduced with the addition of protector into the precursor. In contrast to the large structures for ZnO(1)-ZnO(7), the TEM and SEM images in Fig. 1 and Figure S2 show spherical nanoparticles with an average size of 13.6 ± 3.6 nm for ZnO(8) synthesized in an ethylene glycol medium. ZnO(9) synthesized at a higher precursor concentration than ZnO(8) shows a nanosphere morphology with a larger size.

Thus, microscopic observation reveals that the shape and size of ZnO depends on the precursor concentration, the mixing ratio for the precursor and NaOH or protector (dendrimer), and solvent. The shapes of the products are in the form of flower-like structures, plates or bicones and nanospheres, although Hu et al. [21] reported that the cone shape (400 nm) of ZnO changes from a bicone (1000 nm) to a flower-like structure (2500 nm) with time. Despite the valuable protection ability of dendrimers [22], they were observed to be not active enough for the protection of ZnO in the present research. However, the effect of ethylene glycol medium on particle size is remarkable, and a lower precursor concentration is preferable for the preparation of small and water-dispersible nanoparticles. Thus, ZnO(1), ZnO(2), ZnO(4), and ZnO(8) were selected as representative ZnO nanoparticles for the following studies.

The size and shape of particles strongly depend on the conditions for their chemical production as described above. However, the smallest size of nanoparticles obtained by chemical synthesis is limited, if no protector or stabilizer is present, although protectors or stabilizers sometimes inhibit the properties of nanoparticles. The vapor deposition method is suitable to produce nanoparticles without protectors/stabilizers, but the particle size is still larger than 5 nm [23]. Therefore, another physical method namely femtosecond laser deposition procedure was used [24]. In contrast to the chemical preparation of ZnO using the hydrothermal synthesis method, in this physical ZnO synthesis method, the Zn ion precursor in water is irradiated with a femtosecond laser at different times, and the products are observed by AFM and SEM. Figures S3(a-c) show AFM images at irradiation times of 1 min, 3 min, and 10 min for aqueous alkaline solutions of Zn ions. The particle heights were determined from AFM to be 10.2 ±
0.1
nm
, 10.8 ± 1.6 nm, and 11.3 ±
1.5
nm, respectively, and these sizes are similar to that (18.1 ± 3.1 nm) obtained for ZnO(P10.2) synthesized by the hydrothermal method in ethylene glycol under alkaline conditions (Table S1 and Figure S4). Incidentally, the particle size increases in the order of ZnO(8) < ZnO(9) < ZnO(P10.2) for the same hydrothermal procedure because of the difference in Zn precursor concentration: The lower precursor concentration produces smaller particles (see Table S1). Figure S3(f) shows a SEM image of ZnO(F10.2) taken after 1 min of femtosecond laser irradiation. The image reveals small-sized particles (∼10 nm) similar to that observed in the image of ZnO(8) shown in Fig. 1 .

However, when the femtosecond laser irradiation procedure was applied to the aqueous precursor solutions in water, the reaction showed small products. According to the histogram, the heights of products were 1.2
±
0.1
nm
, 1.40 ± 0.7 nm, and 2.6
±
0.4
nm for 1 min, 3 min, and 10 min of irradiation, respectively (Fig. 2
(a-c)). HRTEM images and size distribution histograms for the products at 1 min and 3 min (Fig. 2 (d) and S3(d,e)) show spherical particles with average sizes of 1.1
±
0.2
nm and 1.5
±
0.3
nm, respectively. Since the vertical height measured by AFM and the diameter determined from HRTEM are similar, the product can be predicted to be nanospheres. The result shown in Fig. 2 e indicates that as the femtosecond laser irradiation time increases, the average height also increases. In this regard, the size (9.4 ± 1.7 nm) of ZnO(P5.5) prepared from the hydrothermal process in ethylene glycol without the addition of NaOH (Table S1 and Figure S4) is smaller than the nanospheres prepared in alkaline ethylene glycol but not as small as nanoparticles prepared under femtosecond laser irradiation in water without the addition of NaOH.

Although the preparation of ZnO by femtosecond laser irradiation was confirmed, the mechanism is not clear: In particular, the difference in reaction behavior in water with and without added NaOH needs to be clarified. With the optical decomposition of water molecules after femtosecond laser irradiation, the generation of hydroxyl radicals
OH
∗
, solvated electrons
e
aq
-
, and hydrogen radicals
H
∗
has been reported [25]. Then, tert-butyl, TEMPO, and DMPO can be used as scavengers for these active species [26]. Fig. 2 (f-h) show AFM images of ZnO(F5.5) produced by femtosecond laser reaction (1 min irradiation) in the presence of scavengers. With the existence of scavengers (tert-butyl, TEMPO, and DMPO, respectively), the numbers of ZnO(F5.5) particles in 4 × 4 µm2 are 81, 84, and 369, and their heights are 0.30 ± 0.20 nm, 0.59
±
0.17
nm ,
and 1.60
±
0.16
nm. In comparison with the case without a scavenger (268 particles and a height of 1.20
±
0.11
nm), the results imply that tert-butyl effectively inhibits the reaction for the production of ZnO(F5.5) than TEMPO and DMPO.

The scavenger test for ZnO(F10.2) production by 1 min femtosecond laser irradiation was performed by weighing the mass of the product on mica. Compared to the ZnO(F10.2) product (6.0 mg) obtained without a scavenger, the products from the reaction solutions including tert-butyl, TEMPO, and DMPO weighed 3 mg, 4.5 mg, and 6.5 mg, respectively. According to these findings, tert-butyl more effectively hinders the femtosecond laser reaction than TEMPO and DMPO for the formation of ZnO(F10.2).

The obtained results under scavengers indicate that the hydroxyl radical
OH
∗
mainly contributes to the femtosecond laser process for both ZnO(F5,5) and ZnO(F10. 2), but the solvated electrons
e
aq
-
and hydrogen radicals
H
∗
do not play the contribution. Since the hydroxyl radical
OH
∗
is a strong reducing agent (E0 = (OH*/H2O) = +2.8 VNHE, where ions or atoms can oxidize to a higher oxidation state) [27], it can act as a strong reducing agent in water. The proposed reaction process as a result of the detection of active species and the ultimate reaction product are then given below, as indicated in Eqs. (1) and (2).
(1)

2 +

Zn ( O H )

For the case of ZnO(F10.2), since
OH
∗
is already provided, reaction (1) proceeds abundantly and, therefore, produces nanoparticles larger than ZnO(F5.5) faster (even at 10 sec).

XRD patterns for ZnO(1, 2, 4, 8) are shown in Fig. 3 , and the Bragg peaks are listed in Table S2. All diffraction peaks can be indexed to ZnO of the wurtzite hexagonal (C6mc space) group (JCPDS 36–1451). Since no impurities such as Zn(OH)2 can be detected in the XRD pattern, it is obvious that the hexagonal phase of ZnO was obtained under the current synthesis. Although the XRD patterns for ZnO(2) are in complete agreement with the wurtzite hexagonal structure, the series of ZnO(1) patterns display slightly higher 2 theta angles. Both series of patterns coexist in ZnO(4), and the broadened wurtzite hexagonal pattern of ZnO(8) is due to the contribution of the second series. Then, the lattice constants (lattice parameters) for both structures were calculated based on Bragg’s law and the equation for the Miller index of a crystal plane using a Jade 6.0 software for multiple plane analysis [28] as summarized in Table S3. The lattice constants for ZnO(2) and ZnO(8) agree with the values expected for the wurtzite hexagonal structure (JCPDS 36–1451), but ZnO(1) and ZnO(4) showed shorter lattice constants, although the lattice constant ratio (c/a) is within the range of 1.595–1.618.

Fig. 3 also shows the XRD patterns obtained for ZnO(P5.5) and ZnO(F10.2). Both XRD patterns exhibit the same Bragg peaks expected for a wurtzite hexagonal structure for ZnO with lattice constants of a = 0.324 nm and c = 0.517 nm (c/a ratio = 1.596), consistent with the wurtzite hexagonal lattice constants. Fig. 3 shows the XRD patterns obtained for the Zn ion precursor under alkaline conditions prior to femtosecond irradiation. Bragg peaks are generated at 2θ = 20.4, 27.9, 32.9, and 40.8°, which correspond to the (110), (201), (211), and (311) planes of zinc hydroxide (JCPDS 38–0385). Zinc hydroxide nitrate produces peaks at 2θ = 47.4°, corresponding to the (−712) plane. Weak (100) and (101) planes at 2θ = 31.7 and 36.2° are also found to coexist. Finally, ZnO nanoparticles with a hexagonal wurtzite structure are produced from Zn(OH)2 with an orthorhombic (wulfingite) structure by femtosecond laser photon energy. Such a reaction can be recognized from the disappearance of the characteristic XRD peaks for the Zn(OH)2 wulfingite structure.

Since ZnO(F5.5) prepared by 1 min or 3 min femtosecond laser irradiation in water shows a size of less than 2 nm, one cannot expect to resolve the Bragg peaks in the XRD pattern because of the strong peak broadening. Thus, the crystal structure of ZnO(F5.5) was observed with HRTEM. Fig. 3 (h, i) show the HRTEM images of ZnO(F5.5) after 1 and 3 min of laser irradiation. The particle sizes are 1.1
±
0.2
nm and 1.5
±
0.3
nm, respectively, which are not in contradiction to the sizes shown in Fig. 2 (d) and Figure S3 (d,e). The d-spacings for ZnO(F5.5) at 1 min of irradiation are observed to be 0.199 nm, 0.241 nm, 0.263 nm, and 0.287 nm corresponding to the (102), (101), (002), and (100) planes, respectively, and the d-spacings for ZnO(F5.5) at 3 min irradiation are observed to be 0.242 nm, 0.264 nm, and 0.289 nm for the (101), (002) and (100) planes, respectively, of the ZnO hexagonal crystal structure. Thus, the HRTEM results confirm the hexagonal crystal structure of ZnO(F5.5).

The chemical compositions in ZnO(F5.5) were also assessed using XPS as shown in Fig. 4 , where full XPS scans and fine analyses including their deconvolution are presented. Full XPS surveys exhibit C 1 s, O 1 s, and Zn 2p peaks. Since ZnO(F5.5) does not include carbon-based species, the C 1 s peaks are attributed to contaminants in ITO, which was used as the conductive substrate for XPS measurements [29]. Then in the fine analysis of the O 1 s peak for ZnO(F5.5), the peak observed at 533.8 eV is assigned to O- 2 of the Zn-O bond which is the main component of the wurtzite structure of ZnO [15]. Zn-OH is the distinct signal due to hydroxyl groups formed on the ZnO surface. The Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 peaks show binding energies of 1023.7 eV and 1045.0 eV, respectively. Thus, XPS analysis confirms the existence of ZnO(F5.5) in the crystal and the appearance of Zn-OH on the surface of the ZnO nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrical and optical properties of the ZnO nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para:
The reflectance spectra for ZnO were measured by fluorescence spectrometry (Figure S5 a). Then, the band gap energy Eg was calculated from the Tauc relation

for a direct band gap material. Then Eg can be obtained from a plot of (αhν)2 versus hν by extrapolating the initial straight line to (αhv)2 = 0 (see Figure S5 b). The band gap energies for ZnO(1), ZnO(2), ZnO(4) and ZnO(8) were determined to be 3.27 eV, 3.24 eV, 3.33 eV and 3.27 eV, respectively. Similarly, Tauc plots for ZnO(F10.2) and ZnO(F5.5), and the obtained band gaps are plotted in Fig. 5
(a,b). Although the band gaps for both ZnO(F10.2) and ZnO(F5.5) are independent of the femtosecond laser irradiation time (from 10 sec to 45 min), the band gap values for ZnO(F10.2) and ZnO(F5.5) are different: The band gap (2.9–3.3 eV) of ZnO(F10.2) is similar to that of ZnO(1,2,4,8) but that of ZnO(F5.5) is very high (5.3–5.6 eV) (Fig. 5 (c)). The difference between ZnO(F10.2) and ZnO(F5.5) can be even observed in the UV–visible absorption spectra (Fig. 5 (d,e)). The absorption band (209–213 nm) of ZnO(F5.5) is 30 times stronger than that of ZnO(F10.2), and the latter reveals additional weaker bands at ∼ 300 nm and 346–362 nm, although these bands occur at wavelengths that are independent of the laser irradiation time (Fig. 5 (f)).

It should be noted that the band gap energy values (5.0–5.6 eV) of ZnO(F5.5) are remarkably high in comparison with those of conventional ZnO. A similar large band gap energy (∼5.0 eV) has been reported for zirconium dioxide (ZrO2), which possesses an active photocatalytic property [32]. Mn2+ doped ZnS d-dots shows band gaps ranging from 5.0 to 5.6 eV [33]. ZnO(F5.5) exhibits a particle size of 1.1
±
0.2
nm and a band gap energy of 5.4 eV (Fig. 5 (c)). Thus, the high band gap of a semiconductor nanoparticle should be inversely proportional to the particle size. As a result of the decrease in particle size of ZnO(F5.5), its band gap can increase.

The conduction bands for ZnO(F5.5), ZnO(P5.5), ZnO(F10.2), and ZnO(P10.2), which are given by the x-axis intercept of the linear portion, are located at −0.60 eV, −0.66 eV, −0.72 eV, and −0.90 eV versus NHE, respectively. Thus, the conductive band potential of ZnO(F5.5) jumps to a more positive value compared to the others samples. This behavior should be attributed to the shift of the Fermi level closer to the lowest conduction band. This means that the electrons can move from the higher conduction band to the lower conduction band.

The following equation can be used to calculate the boundaries of the conduction band (CB) and the valence band (VB) in the ZnO composite [32]:

, Eg and EC denote the valence band energy, conduction band energy, absolute electronegativity of ZnO, band gap energy, and energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale of zinc, respectively. According to the estimated conduction and valence band edges and band gap energies of ZnO(F5.5), the band gap energy is larger because of its smaller crystalline size, as demonstrated by AFM and HRTEM measurements. Using the band gap energy values calculated from the Tauc plots (Fig. 5 (a,b)), the conduction band edge ECB and the valence edge EVB were calculated using Eqs. (6) and (7). The band energy diagram of the materials was then plotted in the form of the redox potential in NHE units in Fig. 5 (h).

The physical characteristics of the series resistance (Rs) and charge transfer resistance (Rct) were obtained by fitting the EIS spectra (Nyquist diagram) to a comparable model (Fig. 6
(a)) and the obtained numerical values are listed in Table 1
. The initial slope of the straight line of the Nyquist plot for the ZnO(F5.5) electrode is larger than that of the ZnO(P5.5), ZnO(F10.2), and ZnO(P10.2) electrodes, indicating a low charge resistance and a long electron lifetime. The internal resistance slightly changes among ZnO nanoparticles, but the charge transfer resistance values decrease in the order of ZnO(P10.2) > ZnO(F10.2) > ZnO(P5.5) > ZnO(F5.5), demonstrating a faster electron transport in accordance with a higher photodegradation efficiency for a short time for the case of ZnO(F5.5).

The electron lifetime
τ e
of the catalysts was calculated from the medium frequency (
f
mid
=
ω
mid
2 π
) from a Bode plot using the relation
τ e
=
1
ω
mid
[17]. The electron lifetimes for ZnO(F5.5), ZnO(P5.5), ZnO(F10.2) and ZnO(P10.2) nanoparticles were calculated to be 40.4 ms, 29.6 ms, 22.1 ms, and 16.5 ms, respectively, as listed in Table 1. Hence, this order is consistent with the estimated order of the electron transfer, and ZnO(F5.5) has the longest electron lifetime. Thus, it can be confirmed that ZnO(F5.5) improves the separation of photogenerated charge carriers due to the larger
τ e
and smaller Rct values. As shown in Fig. 6 (b), peaks appear at low and high frequencies for ZnO(F5.5), ZnO(P5.5) and ZnO(P10.2). The low frequency shift implies that the ZnO on the substrate leads to dielectric leakage because when an electric field is applied, free charges are generated at the droplet interface, providing pathways for the electrolyte to reach the substrate through the holes, while the high frequency implies that the electrolyte cannot reach the substrate through the holes [37]. The shift of the peaks observed in the Bode phase plots for ZnO(F5.5) and ZnO(P5.5) at low frequency indicates an increased electron lifetime, as shown in Fig. 6 (b) and Table 1. Moreover, the decrease in peak height indicates that the reaction becomes less capacitive. This occurs because the pores allow the solution to penetrate the substrate with increasing immersion time [38].

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra for ZnO(F5.5), ZnO(P5.5), ZnO(F10.2), and ZnO(P10.2) are shown in Fig. 6 (c-f). The intensity of the PL spectra decrease in the order of ZnO(F5.5) > ZnO(P5.5) > ZnO(F10.2) > ZnO(P10.2). Although the emission intensity of ZnO(F5.5) decreases with irradiation time, ZnO(F10.2) is always independent of the irradiation time (see Fig. 5 (i)). Comparing this behavior with the size dependence and band gap values for ZnO(F5.5) and ZnO(F10.2), the stronger fluorescence behavior can be related to the small particle size and wide band gap. The increase in fluorescence and band gap with decreasing size is likely due to electron confinement at the nanoscale, the so-called quantum size effect. In other words, the valence and conduction bands shift by +eV and -eV, respectively, resulting in a high band gap [31,32].

The photocatalytic activity can depend on the size and shape of the catalysts. Thus, the photocatalytic degradation of the poisonous organic pollutant, formaldehyde, was characterized using different ZnO nanoparticles under UV light irradiation. Fig. 7
a and 7b show plots of the remaining formaldehyde concentration after photocatalytic degradation against reaction time. Different types of ZnO nanoparticles present different effects on the degradation of formaldehyde. The most effective ZnO nanoparticles are ZnO(8), followed by ZnO(1), ZnO(4) and ZnO(2). When the oxidation is subjected to a 1st order reaction, ln(C0/Ct) =
κ K t =
kappt (for Kt≪1), where C0 is an initial concentration, Ct is a concentration at reaction time t,
κ
is the reaction rate constant, and K is the absorption coefficient. Figure S6 displays good linear relations for lnCt against t with R2 values of 0.96–0.99. The reaction rate constant for ZnO(1), ZnO(2), ZnO(4) and ZnO(8) was determined to be 1.038 h−1, 0.269 h−1, 0.354 h−1, and 1.315 h−1, respectively. It can be confirmed from these values that the photocatalytic activities of the ZnO nanoparticles follow the order of ZnO(8) > ZnO(1) > ZnO(4) > ZnO(2). This order can be compared to the morphologies and surface properties of the catalysts as listed in Table 2 . ZnO(8) shows the smallest size (13.6 nm), largest surface area (53.24 m2/g), and largest total pore volume (0.247 cm3/g) in comparison with the other samples (see Table 2). Since ZnO(1) shows to have the largest particle size but exhibits a secondary larger surface area and total pore volume because of its flower-like shape, the catalytic reaction was secondarily high. Although ZnO(4) and ZnO(2) have sphere and plate shapes, respectively, and the particle size is smaller than that of ZnO(1), their surface area and pore volume are smaller than that of ZnO(8) and ZnO(1); thus, the catalytic efficiency is lower. This may originate from their compact crystal structure as evident in the sharp XRD peaks observed for these materials. Therefore, the band gap is independent of the catalytic efficiency.

The dependence of particle size in addition to shape on catalytic activity was further confirmed for different sizes of ZnO(F5.5) nanoparticles, alongside ZnO(P5.5) and ZnO(F10.2) nanoparticles. Fig. 7 b shows that the degradation of formaldehyde upon 1 min laser irradiation of ZnO(F5.5) is completed within 15 min. However, the formaldehyde conversion on ZnO(F5.5) produced at 3 min and 10 min laser irradiation takes 25 min and 37.5 min, respectively, although these reaction times are shorter than those obtained for ZnO(P5.5) and ZnO(F10.2). This indicates that a smaller nanoparticle is more effective than larger particles for catalytic activity. As shown in Fig. 7 c, the linearity of the first-order reaction equation is good at earlier times. Table 3 displays the apparent rate constant (kapp) with variation R2. The kinetics rate constants decrease in the order of ZnO(F5.5 (1 min)) > ZnO(F5.5 (3 min)) > ZnO(F5.5 (10 min)) > ZnO(P5.5) > ZnO(F10.2) > ZnO(8). It can be observed from these results that the reaction depends on the particle size. The particle size also affects to the surface area of the particles, as given in Table 2. The surface area of ZnO(F10.2) is slightly larger than that of ZnO(8), but that of ZnO(F5.5) is far larger than both of these particles. This order is consistent with the kinetics rate constant. These results imply that the high photocatalytic activity of ZnO(F5.5) can be ascribed to its small particle size, large surface area, low charge-transfer resistance, and high electron lifetime for photogenerated holes and electrons. Since electrons live longer, the electron counterpart (h+) also lives longer and plays a role in hydroxyl radical formation (Eq. (9)) and the catalytic reaction (Eq. (10)). Thus, ZnO(F5.5) is a promising choice for photocatalytic formaldehyde detoxification due to its excellent efficiency.

Comparing the reaction time, the importance of size should be clear. Even if the particle size is only 20 nm, the reaction takes 240 min but a particle with a 1 nm particle size takes only 15 min to react. Darvishi Cheshmeh Soltani et al. [39] found that the photocatalytic degradation of formaldehyde is completed at 120 min (kapp = 0.0265 min−1) using ZnO immobilized on glass plates, although size and the shape of nanoparticles were not clarified. Platinum is the best catalyst, but platinum protected by dendrimers and incorporated into porous materials reveals a long reaction time despite the platinum particles being very small (∼2 nm) [40]. When platinum nanoparticle-incorporated dendrimer is chemically bound to a cellulose nanofiber film, formaldehyde is trapped on the film, but the decomposition of formaldehyde taken several hours [20]. These results highlight the excellent catalytic efficiency of bare particles and their small size.

The recyclability of the regenerated ZnO(F5.5(1 min)) photocatalyst was investigated as shown in Fig. 7 d. The photocatalytic formaldehyde reduction efficiency for the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd cycles of UV light irradiation was found to be 98.49%, 97.99%, and 96.03%, respectively. This performance proves that ZnO(F5.5(1 min)) is stable and reusable.

UV irradiation promotes the formation of electron-hole pairs that charge
OH
-
to
OH
∗
(hydroxyl radical), and O2 to
O
2
∗ -
(superoxide radical anion).
O
2
∗ -
and
OH
∗
can decompose pollutants on the ZnO surface [41]. The activation of ZnO is achieved through the absorption of a photon with ultraband energy from a UV irradiation source. This leads to the transport of an electron (e−) from the valence band to the conduction band and creates highly reactive positive holes (h+) in the valence band. This leads to the aggressive oxidation of toxic organic pollutants (formaldehyde) on the surface and converts them to CO2 and H2O [42]. The trapping of effective species was studied by determining the active species involved in the photocatalytic process. This investigation was carried out by introducing effective scavengers (t-BuOH, p-benzoquinone, and KI for
OH
∗
,
O
2
∗ -
and h+, respectively) into the photodegradation process [36]. As shown in Fig. 7 e, all scavengers inhibit the decomposition process of formaldehyde by ZnO(F5.5(1 min)) in the order of t-BuOH > KI
≈
p-benzoquinone. This result implies that
OH
∗
participation in the reaction is preferable. Thus, the related main reactions for formaldehyde destruction on the ZnO catalyst can be described by the catalytic mechanism following Eqs. (8–10). The responsible process is illustrated in Scheme 7f.

In this study, ZnO nanoparticles with different particle sizes and shapes were synthesized and characterized. While the mechanical and hydrothermal methods lead to the production of larger ZnO nanoparticles, the nanoparticles formed using a femtosecond pulse laser irradiation method are smaller. The particle growth for ZnO(F5.5) during 1–30 min of laser irradiation was then compared to ZnO(F10.2) because a smaller amount of
OH
∗
is supplied at acidic (5.5) pH. Similarly, the size of ZnO(P5.5) is smaller than that of ZnO(P10.2). ZnO(F5.5) shows a wider band gap, a lower charge transfer resistance, a longer electron life time, and a higher emission intensity than ZnO(P5.5), ZnO(F10.2), and ZnO(P10.2). These results indicate the quantum property of ZnO(F5.5) nanoparticles. A small particle size is particularly advantageous for achieving the rapid separation of photoexcited pairs into electrons and holes in conductive nanoparticles. The production of lower mass particles (1 nm in size) after short (1 min) femtosecond laser irradiation is due to the contribution of hydroxyl radicals
OH
∗
, which are strong reducing agents in water. The fluorescence emission from ZnO(F5.5) is higher than that from other ZnO, indicating the property of quantum clusters/nanoparticles. Moreover, the photocatalytic activity of ZnO(F5.5) is also noteworthy. The photocatalytic reaction times for ZnO(F5.5) for formaldehyde are shorter than those for ZnO(P5.5) and ZnO (F10.2). This indicates that a smaller nanoparticle is more effective than larger particles. Thus, femtosecond laser irradiation is a better technique for the fabrication of clusters/nanoparticles with excellent semiconducting, electronic, photocatalytic, and quantum properties than nanoparticles prepared using conventional methods.
